meta.name: Extraskeletal Myxoid Chondrosarcoma
meta.summary: A rare soft-tissue sarcoma (about 1-3% of all soft-tissue sarcomas) defined by recurrent NR4A3 gene rearrangements (most commonly EWSR1::NR4A3). Despite the name it is not related to cartilage/bone chondrosarcoma. It is usually slow-growing but has a high long-term rate of local recurrence and distant metastasis (especially to the lungs), so long-term follow-up matters.
meta.reviewedBy: Initial automated draft from published literature - NOT yet clinician-reviewed
meta.patientFriendlyOneLiner: A rare, usually slow-growing sarcoma that can come back or spread years later, so the long game (good surgery + long follow-up) is what matters most.

overview.whatItIs: Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma (EMC) is a malignant soft-tissue tumour with a distinctive myxoid (gel-like) matrix and cords of rounded cells. The 'chondrosarcoma' in the name is historical and misleading: modern molecular studies show EMC does not show true cartilage differentiation and is biologically unrelated to skeletal chondrosarcoma.
overview.howCommon: Roughly 1-3% of all soft-tissue sarcomas. Estimated incidence is well under 1 per million per year, which is why information is so hard to find.
overview.typicalPatient: Most often diagnosed in adults in their 50s-60s, but it occurs across a wide age range including young adults. Slight male predominance (~2:1 in several series).
commonSites: Deep soft tissue of the thigh and lower limb (most common)
Less commonly upper limb, head and neck, retroperitoneum
overview.molecular: EMC is defined by rearrangement of the NR4A3 gene on chromosome 9q22. The most common fusion is EWSR1::NR4A3 (~62-75% of cases). Variant fusions include TAF15::NR4A3, TCF12::NR4A3, TFG::NR4A3 and FUS::NR4A3. Non-EWSR1 (variant) fusions are associated more often with high-grade/rhabdoid morphology and a more aggressive course.
overview.howDiagnosed: Diagnosis is made by an expert sarcoma pathologist using image-guided core-needle biopsy, immunohistochemistry, and confirmation of an NR4A3 rearrangement by FISH or RNA sequencing. MRI is the main imaging for the primary tumour; CT chest is used to look for lung metastases.
keyPointsForNewlyDiagnosed: Get your pathology reviewed at a specialist sarcoma centre - EMC is frequently misdiagnosed.
Ask whether your tumour's NR4A3 fusion partner was identified (EWSR1 vs a variant) - it carries prognostic information.
EMC tends to be indolent but recurs/metastasises over many years, so follow-up is a marathon, not a sprint.
Complete surgical removal with clear margins is the single most important treatment for localised disease.

studies.intro: A curated, growing list of published literature on EMC. Use the search links to find newly published papers, then add them here. Flag = our confidence the link/citation is correct.

items.title: From pathogenesis to the patient's bedside: a comprehensive review of extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma
items.journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
items.notes: Recent comprehensive review covering biology, prognosis and management. Good starting point.

items.title: Pazopanib for treatment of advanced extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma (GEIS/ISG/FSG): a multicentre, single-arm, phase 2 trial
items.notes: Key prospective trial: pazopanib showed activity (ORR ~18%, median PFS ~19 months); responses concentrated in EWSR1::NR4A3-positive tumours.

items.title: Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma: Clinical features and overall survival
items.notes: Analysis of clinical features and overall survival; identifies prognostic factors including age, grade, size, location and metastatic spread.

items.title: Extraskeletal Myxoid Chondrosarcoma: Clinical and Molecular Characteristics and Outcomes of Patients Treated at Two Institutions
items.notes: Two-institution cohort; size >10 cm and metastasis at presentation were independently prognostic.

items.title: Extraskeletal Myxoid Chondrosarcoma: State of the Art and Current Research on Biology and Clinical Management
items.notes: Open-access review of biology and management.

items.title: Results of a sub-analysis of a phase 2 study on trabectedin treatment for EMC and mesenchymal chondrosarcoma
items.notes: Trabectedin showed disease control; median PFS reported ~12.5 months in the trabectedin group.

items.title: Extraskeletal Myxoid Chondrosarcoma: Long-Term Survival in the Setting of Metastatic Disease
items.notes: Illustrates that long-term survival is possible even with metastatic EMC due to its indolent biology.

items.title: Diagnosis of extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma in the thigh using EWSR1-NR4A3 gene fusion: a case report
items.notes: Example of molecular confirmation by EWSR1-NR4A3 fusion detection.

items.title: Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma (topic review)
items.notes: Concise pathology reference with diagnostic criteria and references.

outcomes.intro: Published outcome data for EMC, presented the way an outcomes study would present it. Because EMC is so rare, most numbers come from small series, two-institution cohorts and population (SEER-type) analyses. Ranges reflect genuine differences between studies.

prognosticFactors.note: Independently associated with worse survival in multiple series.

prognosticFactors.factor: High histologic grade / cellularity / rhabdoid features
Metastatic or nodal disease at presentation

prognosticFactors.note: No surgery is associated with worse outcomes.
Associated with high-grade morphology and more aggressive behaviour.

treatmentResponse.result: ORR ~18%, median PFS ~19 months; responses concentrated in EWSR1::NR4A3-positive disease
Disease control with median PFS ~12.5 months in a phase 2 sub-analysis

registry.intro: This tool pools two kinds of real, cited evidence to explore 'what happened to people like me?': grouped outcomes from published EMC cohorts/registries and individual case reports. Matching studies are summed into a crude top-line (with confidence intervals) and every contributing study is listed beneath it. Each row shows when its patients were diagnosed, because older data can understate today's outlook. Where studies genuinely disagree, see 'Where the evidence disagrees' below rather than trusting a single pooled number.
registry.dataStatusBanner: These numbers are pooled from REAL published EMC cohorts and individual case reports, each linked to its source - not synthetic data. The top-line figures are CRUDE pooled proportions (with Wilson 95% confidence intervals) across heterogeneous studies of different countries, eras and follow-up - not a survival model. Some large series are shown for context only, to avoid double-counting overlapping populations. See METHODOLOGY.md. Treat everything as a rough signal, never a personal prognosis.

fields.source: Citation for this row (required for real data)
fields.note: Free-text context for this case (not shown in the table)

patients.note: Dual-site abdominal-wall EMC at prior laparoscopic port sites (likely seeding); marginal excision, no adjuvant therapy; NED at 1.8 yr. Age reported only as 'in his 40s' (recorded as decade midpoint).

Paediatric EMC with rhabdoid features; lung metastasis at diagnosis (resected); surgery + ifosfamide/doxorubicin + RT 60 Gy; local relapse at 16 mo, re-excised, then lost to follow-up. Clinical size 13x17 cm, specimen 10x11.5x7.5 cm.
Primary intracranial (right frontal) EMC; high-grade with INI1 loss and Ki-67 80%; gross total resection + RT 66 Gy/33 fx, no chemo; only 3-month follow-up so far.
EMC around the left knee initially mistaken for myoepithelial tumour; TAF15::NR4A3 confirmed by RNA sequencing; extracapsular resection + distal femoral replacement, no adjuvant therapy; NED at 3 yr.

cohorts.label: Localised at diagnosis, surgically treated
cohorts.note: Prognostic subset; 8 non-surgically-treated localised cases excluded. R1/R2 margins were the main risk factor for recurrence.

cohorts.label: Metastatic at diagnosis
cohorts.note: 27 lung, 2 peritoneal metastases; 16 received systemic therapy. Metastasis is the inclusion criterion here, so it is not counted as a metastasis outcome.

cohorts.label: Localised, single-institution series
cohorts.note: 10-yr local control 100% with surgery+RT vs 63% surgery alone. Likely overlaps US SEER, so not pooled.

cohorts.label: Localised, US SEER population
cohorts.dssText: 94% vs 85% (5-yr CSS, RT vs no RT)
cohorts.note: 94% had surgery, 32% radiotherapy. Population registry does not capture recurrence.

cohorts.label: Local recurrence by surgical margin
cohorts.note: Shows how strongly margin status drives local recurrence.

cohorts.label: Advanced disease on pazopanib
cohorts.primaryRef: Stacchiotti et al., Lancet Oncol 2019 phase 2 (n=26)
cohorts.note: Objective response 18%, median PFS 19 months; better with EWSR1::NR4A3. Treatment-response context.

treatments.intro: General, plain-language summary of how EMC is typically managed, organised by situation. This is educational only and is based on published reviews and ESMO/NCCN soft-tissue sarcoma principles - it is not a treatment recommendation for any individual. Decisions must be made with a sarcoma multidisciplinary team (MDT).
principles: EMC should be managed by a specialist sarcoma multidisciplinary team from the start.
Surgery with clear (R0) margins is the backbone of treatment for disease that has not spread.
Because EMC is relatively chemo-resistant, systemic therapy is generally reserved for advanced/metastatic or progressive disease.

byStage.label: Localised disease (tumour confined to where it started)
options: Wide local excision aiming for clear (R0) margins - the most important treatment.
Radiotherapy considered before or after surgery when margins are close/threatened or for large or high-grade tumours.
Systemic chemotherapy is generally NOT routine for localised EMC; discuss within an MDT or trial.
Confirm NR4A3 fusion status as part of work-up.

byStage.label: Locally advanced / regional disease
options: MDT planning for resectability; neoadjuvant radiotherapy +/- systemic therapy to enable surgery.
Surgery if the tumour can be removed with acceptable function.

byStage.label: Metastatic / advanced disease (most often lung)
options: EMC can be indolent even when metastatic - watchful waiting may be appropriate for slow, asymptomatic disease.
Surgical removal (metastasectomy) of a small number of lung metastases may be considered.
Pazopanib has EMC-specific activity (ORR ~18%, median PFS ~19 months), especially in EWSR1::NR4A3-positive disease.
Anthracycline-based chemotherapy is generally used first for symptomatic or rapidly progressive disease.
Trabectedin has shown disease control in EMC.

treatments.disclaimer: Drug names and sequencing reflect published evidence and guidelines at the time of review and may be out of date. Availability differs by country. Always defer to your treating sarcoma team.

emergingTreatments.intro: Newer and investigational approaches being studied for EMC. These are NOT standard of care - some are supported only by small series or case reports, and some are available only inside clinical trials. They are listed so you can raise them with your sarcoma team and ask whether a trial fits you. No drug specifically targets the defining NR4A3 fusion yet.
emergingTreatments.disclaimer: Investigational. Inclusion here is not a recommendation and does not mean a treatment is proven, approved, or available to you. Discuss any of these with your sarcoma specialist, ideally through a clinical trial.

items.name: Anlotinib (multi-target TKI: VEGFR/FGFR/PDGFR/c-Kit)
items.status: Investigational for EMC; approved in China for some advanced soft-tissue sarcomas
items.summary: In a phase IIB soft-tissue sarcoma trial, anlotinib improved median progression-free survival vs placebo (6.27 vs 1.47 months). EMC-specific data are limited, but its anti-angiogenic mechanism is similar to pazopanib.

items.name: Sunitinib (multi-target TKI)
items.status: Off-label; small series / case reports in EMC
items.summary: Reported tumour responses in a subset of EMC patients, supporting the role of anti-angiogenic TKIs in this disease.

items.name: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g. nivolumab), alone or with a TKI
items.summary: EMC often has an immunosuppressive tumour microenvironment, and responses to immunotherapy have so far been occasional rather than reliable. Being studied in combination strategies.

items.name: Genomic + tumour-microenvironment (TME) profiling to guide therapy
items.summary: Next-generation sequencing and TME profiling are being explored to match individual EMC patients to systemic or immune-based therapy, including immune-based treatment selected by gene profiling.

items.name: NR4A3 fusion-directed strategies
items.summary: The EWSR1/TAF15/FUS::NR4A3 fusion is the defining driver of EMC, but there is no clinically validated drug that targets it directly yet. An active area of laboratory research.

clinicalTrials.intro: How to find and join trials. Because EMC is so rare, there are rarely EMC-only trials open at any moment - most relevant studies are broader soft-tissue-sarcoma trials or genomically-matched 'basket' trials that EMC can qualify for. The live links below always show the current list (they update automatically), so use them rather than relying on a static list.
howToEnroll: Ask your oncologist or a specialist sarcoma centre which open trials you might fit - they can refer you, and centres of excellence usually run or know the relevant trials.
Search the live links above (filter for 'recruiting'). Note the trial's identifier (e.g. an NCT number) and its eligibility criteria.
Have your records ready: pathology with NR4A3 fusion status, recent imaging, prior treatments, and current bloodwork - most eligibility decisions hinge on these.
Contact the trial's study coordinator (listed on the trial page) to check eligibility and ask about travel, costs, and reimbursement.
Ask about expanded access / compassionate use if you don't qualify for a trial but a drug looks promising for you.
Get a second opinion at a high-volume sarcoma centre before starting - they may have trials not listed elsewhere.
clinicalTrials.trialsNote: A hand-curated list of specific trials can be added here, but it goes stale fast - the live search links above are the reliable source. If you add specific trials, include the registry ID, status, and a verified link, and re-check them regularly.

monitoring.intro: Suggested follow-up framework after treatment, based on general sarcoma surveillance principles. EMC recurs and metastasises LATE, so follow-up should continue for many years - often 10+ - longer than for many other cancers. Confirm your personal schedule with your sarcoma team.

schedule.imaging: Clinical exam + imaging of the primary site (MRI/ultrasound) + CT chest (lungs are the main site of metastasis)
Clinical exam + primary-site imaging + chest imaging
Clinical exam + chest imaging; primary-site imaging as advised. Do NOT stop follow-up early - late recurrence is common.

whatTheyWatchFor: New lump or change at the original site (local recurrence)
Less commonly spread to soft tissue, bone or other organs
selfMonitoring: Report any new or growing lump, new pain, or breathlessness promptly.
Keep your own copy of pathology (including fusion status), operative and imaging reports.
monitoring.disclaimer: Intervals are illustrative and not a substitute for the personalised plan from your sarcoma centre.

supportGroups.intro: There is no large EMC-only community because the disease is so rare, so the most useful communities are the broader sarcoma networks (which include EMC patients) plus disease-specific Facebook groups. If you run or know of an EMC-specific group, please add it.

groups.name: Sarcoma Patient Advocacy Global Network (SPAGN)
groups.notes: Umbrella of 60+ national sarcoma patient organisations across 5 continents - good way to find a group in your country.

groups.name: Smart Patients - Sarcoma community
groups.notes: Active peer-to-peer discussion forum for patients and families.

groups.name: List of Facebook sarcoma/rare-cancer patient groups
groups.notes: Crowd-maintained list of Facebook groups; search it for EMC / myxoid chondrosarcoma.

groups.name: r/sarcoma
groups.notes: General sarcoma subreddit; search for EMC threads or post to find others.

groups.name: SARC - patient advocacy directory
groups.notes: Links to advocacy and support organisations.

supportGroups.wanted: An EMC-specific Facebook/Discord group does not appear to be widely listed. If you start or find one, please contribute it here.

centers.intro: Major specialist sarcoma centres. Sarcoma outcomes are better when care is delivered at high-volume centres, so getting your case reviewed at one of these (or one near you) is worthwhile. The 'Find a specialist near me' tool below uses these locations. This list is a starting set of well-known centres, NOT an exhaustive or ranked list, and inclusion is not an endorsement - verify current EMC expertise directly.

list.name: Istituto Nazionale dei Tumori (INT)
list.notes: Led key EMC-specific trials (pazopanib); strong EMC expertise.

list.name: Gustave Roussy
list.notes: Leading French sarcoma centre (French Sarcoma Group).

list.name: Duke Sarcoma Center
list.notes: SARC member; NCI comprehensive cancer center.

questionsForOncologist.intro: Questions worth asking your oncologist / sarcoma team. Bring a written copy and a friend to take notes.

questions: Has my pathology been reviewed by an expert sarcoma pathologist?
Was my diagnosis molecularly confirmed - which NR4A3 fusion do I have (EWSR1 vs a variant), and what does that mean for me?
What grade and size is my tumour, and where exactly is it?
Is my tumour completely removable with clear margins, and what would surgery mean for function?
Do I need radiotherapy before or after surgery?
Is chemotherapy or a targeted drug (e.g. pazopanib) relevant for me now or only if it spreads?
Are there clinical trials I'm eligible for, here or elsewhere?
How many EMC (or sarcoma) patients does this centre treat per year?
Given my specifics, what are realistic outcomes and what are the chances of recurrence or spread?
What will my follow-up schedule be, and for how many years?
What symptoms should make me contact you between appointments?
Can you refer me to a sarcoma specialist centre for a second opinion?
What support, rehab, fertility, or mental-health resources are available to me?
Can my tumour tissue/data be banked for research?

evidenceQuestions.question: Does adjuvant or neoadjuvant radiotherapy improve outcomes in localized EMC?
evidenceQuestions.summary: Single-institution and population studies suggest radiotherapy improves local control (and possibly survival), while a large national registry and other cohorts found no effect on local recurrence. Much of both the apparent benefit and the apparent harm reflects indication bias: radiotherapy is preferentially given to higher-risk tumours (close or positive margins, large or truncal lesions). No randomized trials exist.

positions.claim: 10-year local control 100% with surgery + radiotherapy vs 63% with surgery alone (p=0.004); surgery alone carried HR 12.7 for local recurrence.
positions.caveat: Combined-modality patients differ systematically from surgery-alone patients; single centre.

positions.claim: External-beam radiotherapy associated with higher cancer-specific survival (5-yr CSS 94% vs 85%, p=0.01).
positions.caveat: Observational; treatment not randomized; residual confounding likely.

positions.claim: No association between (neo)adjuvant radiotherapy and local recurrence (HR 0.50, 95% CI 0.11-2.25; p=0.37). RT recipients far more often had positive margins (42% vs 18%).
positions.caveat: Indication bias toward higher-risk tumours; RT even appeared as an adverse disease-specific-survival factor on univariate analysis for the same reason.

positions.claim: No difference in local recurrence with vs without radiotherapy (41% vs 35%, p=0.79).

evidenceQuestions.bottomLine: Guideline-pragmatic practice (extrapolated from ESMO/NCCN soft-tissue-sarcoma guidance) is SELECTIVE radiotherapy for close/positive margins or large/truncal tumours, not routine radiotherapy for all localized EMC. The data cannot prove radiotherapy helps unselected patients, and the apparent survival 'harm' from radiotherapy is an artifact of it being given to worse-prognosis disease - not evidence that it is harmful.

evidenceQuestions.question: Does adjuvant or neoadjuvant chemotherapy improve outcomes in localized EMC?
evidenceQuestions.summary: Multiple cohorts and a population analysis agree that perioperative chemotherapy does not measurably improve metastasis-free or overall survival in localized EMC, which is generally chemoresistant. (This is separate from the palliative use of systemic therapy in advanced disease - see Treatments.)

positions.claim: No association between (neo)adjuvant chemotherapy and distant metastasis (HR 0.57, p=0.58) or disease-specific survival.
positions.design: national registry, n=134 localized (6 received chemo)
positions.caveat: Very few treated patients; underpowered for a small effect.

positions.claim: 7 patients given preoperative chemotherapy had no complete pathological responses and no significant improvement in disease-free or overall survival.
Chemotherapy did not improve overall survival and was associated with worse outcomes (HR up to 2.54 in surgically treated patients).
positions.caveat: Strong selection bias - sicker patients receive chemotherapy; also spans a very long, dated era.

evidenceQuestions.bottomLine: Routine adjuvant/neoadjuvant chemotherapy is not recommended for localized EMC. Cytotoxic and anti-angiogenic systemic therapy has a role in advanced/metastatic disease (covered under Treatments and Emerging Treatments), which is a different question.
